children.title: What is the Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics?
children.summary: Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics is offered by Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Engineering (FAIE). The programme page lists registration (R/0788/6/00177) 01/31 and accreditation (MQA/SWA14238). The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.
children.content:
Q: What is the Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics?
A: Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics is offered by Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Engineering (FAIE). The programme page lists registration (R/0788/6/00177) 01/31 and accreditation (MQA/SWA14238). The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.

children.title: What BYOC elective subjects are offered in March/April for Intelligent Robotics?
children.summary: March/April BYOC elective subjects include: Fundamentals of Marketing, Digital Transformation Strategy, Personal Finance, Radio Network Planning Towards 5G, Media Anthropology, Project Management, Motion Capture, Media Law, Corporate Strategy, Social Media Strategies, Introductory Mobile Application Development, Basic Filmmaking, Fundamental of Wireless Communications. A practical way to choose: 1. Pick Project Management or Corporate Strategy if you want stronger project leadership. 2. Pick Introductory Mobile Application Development if you want to connect robotics with apps. 3. Pick Radio Network Planning Towards 5G or Fundamental of Wireless Communications if you like connected robots and networks. 4. Pick Motion Capture or Basic Filmmaking if you want creative or interactive robotics work. 5. Pick Personal Finance if you want a useful life skill outside the technical track.
children.content:
Q: What BYOC elective subjects are offered in March/April for Intelligent Robotics?
A: March/April BYOC elective subjects include: Fundamentals of Marketing, Digital Transformation Strategy, Personal Finance, Radio Network Planning Towards 5G, Media Anthropology, Project Management, Motion Capture, Media Law, Corporate Strategy, Social Media Strategies, Introductory Mobile Application Development, Basic Filmmaking, Fundamental of Wireless Communications. A practical way to choose: 1. Pick Project Management or Corporate Strategy if you want stronger project leadership. 2. Pick Introductory Mobile Application Development if you want to connect robotics with apps. 3. Pick Radio Network Planning Towards 5G or Fundamental of Wireless Communications if you like connected robots and networks. 4. Pick Motion Capture or Basic Filmmaking if you want creative or interactive robotics work. 5. Pick Personal Finance if you want a useful life skill outside the technical track.

children.title: What BYOC elective subjects are offered in October/November for Intelligent Robotics?
children.summary: October/November BYOC elective subjects include: Design Thinking for Strategic Communication, Corporate Communication, Suspenseful Filmmaking, Communications Networks, Introductory Data Science, Introductory Data Visualization, Visual and Corporate Identity, Information Visualization, Principal of Finance, Fundamental of Marketing. A practical way to choose: 1. Pick Introductory Data Science or Introductory Data Visualization if you want stronger data skills for robotics. 2. Pick Communications Networks if you are interested in connected systems. 3. Pick Design Thinking for Strategic Communication if you want better problem framing and presentation skills. 4. Pick Corporate Communication or Visual and Corporate Identity if you want stronger communication for projects. 5. Pick Principal of Finance or Fundamental of Marketing if you want broader business awareness.
children.content:
Q: What BYOC elective subjects are offered in October/November for Intelligent Robotics?
A: October/November BYOC elective subjects include: Design Thinking for Strategic Communication, Corporate Communication, Suspenseful Filmmaking, Communications Networks, Introductory Data Science, Introductory Data Visualization, Visual and Corporate Identity, Information Visualization, Principal of Finance, Fundamental of Marketing. A practical way to choose: 1. Pick Introductory Data Science or Introductory Data Visualization if you want stronger data skills for robotics. 2. Pick Communications Networks if you are interested in connected systems. 3. Pick Design Thinking for Strategic Communication if you want better problem framing and presentation skills. 4. Pick Corporate Communication or Visual and Corporate Identity if you want stronger communication for projects. 5. Pick Principal of Finance or Fundamental of Marketing if you want broader business awareness.

children.summary: Career prospects include: Robotics System Designer/Programmers, AI and Machine Learning Developer, Embedded System Designer, Control and Automation Specialist, Field Application Technologist, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Designer, Production and Planning Engineer, Industry 4.0 Technologist.
children.content:
Q: What career prospects are listed for Intelligent Robotics graduates?
A: Career prospects include: Robotics System Designer/Programmers, AI and Machine Learning Developer, Embedded System Designer, Control and Automation Specialist, Field Application Technologist, Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Designer, Production and Planning Engineer, Industry 4.0 Technologist.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARM6113 Technical Calculus.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARM6113

Title: Technical Calculus

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 48 hours (Lecture Hours = 35 and Supervised Tutorial Hours = 13)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide necessary grounding in calculus, algebra, vectors, complex functions, Fourier analysis and probability to students pursuing engineering and science degrees.

Assessment:
- Tests/Quizzes: 40%
- Final Examination: 60%

Course Contents:
- Calculus of one variable
- Fourier series
- Complex number functions and vector algebra
- Probability

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Compute the limits, derivatives, integrals and extrema of single-variable functions.
- Perform basic vector operations and calculate the powers and roots of complex numbers.
- Construct the power series and the Fourier series representations of functions.
- Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARL6113 Electrical Circuits.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARL6113

Title: Electrical Circuits

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 46 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide a basic understanding of the laws of circuit theory and the behaviour of circuits during steady-state and transient conditions.

Assessment:
- Test/Quiz: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab: 10%
- Final Examination: 50%

Laboratory:
- CK1: Thevenin’s Theorem and Superposition Theorem
- CK2: AC Circuits

Course Contents:
- Circuit elements and signal waveforms
- Basic laws of electric circuit
- Network analysis
- Sinusoidal steady state analysis
- Transient analysis

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain the basic concepts and laws of electric circuits, and different types of signals.
- Solve the electrical networks using nodal and mesh analysis techniques.
- Compute the impedance, resonance and complex power of sinusoidal circuits.
- Analyse the transient response of RL, RC and RLC circuits.
- Construct a circuit by using circuit theory experiment board to verify the law of electric circuit and phasor relationship.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARE6113 Basic Electronics.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARE6113

Title: Basic Electronics

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 47 hours (lectures, tutorials & laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide a sound understanding of basic electronic devices such as diodes, transistors, and their respective applications.

Assessment:
- Lab: 10%
- Assignment: 15%
- Test/Quiz: 15%
- Final Examination: 60%

Laboratory:
- Diode Circuits
- Transistor Circuits

Course Contents:
- Fundamentals of semiconductor devices
- P-N junction characteristics and diode models
- Diode applications
- Bipolar junction transistor and biasing techniques
- Transistor amplifier and small-signal equivalent circuits

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain the fundamental properties of semiconductor material and characteristics of PN junction as circuit element.
- Analyse the diode and bipolar junction transistor (BJT) circuits.
- Analyse the transistor amplifier circuits.
- Demonstrate the diode and transistor circuits.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: What is ARM6123 Differential Equations about?
children.summary: ARM6123 Differential Equations is a core subject in the technical group. Objective: To introduce students to differential equations with focus on the ordinary differential equations and partial differential equations, as well as the requisite concepts and analysis tools to understand and solve them, including partial differentiation and integral transforms.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARM6123 Differential Equations.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARM6123

Title: Differential Equations

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 47 hours (Lecture Hours = 34 and Supervised Tutorial Hours = 13)

Objective:
To introduce students to differential equations with focus on the ordinary differential equations and partial differential equations, as well as the requisite concepts and analysis tools to understand and solve them, including partial differentiation and integral transforms.

- Tests/Quizzes: 40%
- Final Examination: 60%

Course Contents:
- Ordinary differential equations
- Partial differentiation
- Integral transform
- Partial differential equations

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Solve differential equations using various methods.
- Apply concepts of calculus to solve multivariable problems.
- Determine appropriate integral transforms to solve boundary or initial value problem, and difference equations.

Subject Code: ARE6123

Title: Digital Design

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2026

- Design modular combinational circuits using encoders, decoders, multiplexers and demultiplexers.

Subject Code: ARE6133

Title: Analog Electronics

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2026

- Assignment: 15%

Subject Code: MMD6123

Title: Digital Fabrication and Prototyping

Aliases: MID6113, Rapid Modelling

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 3

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARL6134 Electromagnetics with Applications.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARL6134

Title: Electromagnetics with Applications

Aliases: Electromagnetics With Applications

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2027

Credit Hours: 4

Contact Hours: 59 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide an introduction to electromagnetic theory and its applications related to electronics, electrical machine and telecommunication.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 30%
- Lab Assessment: 10%
- Final exam: 40%

Course Contents:
- Electrostatics and electric currents
- Magnetostatics
- Electromagnetic induction
- Electromagnetic wave
- Antennas

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply basic theories of electrostatics and electric currents.
- Apply magnetostatics principles to electrical machines.
- Build an application utilizing electrostatics and electromagnetic induction.
- Demonstrate the characteristics of electromagnetic wave propagation in isotropic mediums.
- Evaluate the practical antenna parameters.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.summary:
Subject Code: ARC6144

Title: Machine Vision & Image Processing

Aliases: Machine Vision and Image Processing

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 4

Contact Hours: 61 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To introduce the basic and advanced concepts and working principles of machine vision and image processing.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Assessment: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Laboratory:
Computer lab, Matlab/Scilab/Octive software, Arduino, Raspbery Pi, OpenCV

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Fundamental of image processing
- Image segmentation
- Image representation and recognition
- Video processing
- Machine vision hardware

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain the fundamental concepts of machine vision, image processing and video processing.
- Design suitable image segmentation, representation and recognition techniques according to a prescribed problem.
- Build a prescribed machine vision system by applying software and hardware design knowledge.

Key contents: Introduction to machine learning; Machine learning workflow; Supervised learning; Unsupervised learning Learning outcomes: Explain the concepts of machine learning algorithms.; Propose solution to real-world problems using related good practices from literatures in machine learning technologies.; Develop machine learning systems and applications.; Demonstrate the application of machine learning technologies in solving real-world problems.; Demonstrate leadership, autonomy and responsibility by collaboratively solving a practical problem.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARR6163 Robot Programming.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARR6163

Title: Robot Programming

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: ARC6113 Computer and Programming

Objective:
To acquire the knowledge, skills and working practices in developing robotic systems and applications with robot programming

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Assessment: 20%
- Project: 40%

Course Contents:
- Robot programming environment
- Modeling, simulation and visualization
- Sensing, vision and depth
- Kinematics and control
- Localization, mapping and SLAM
- Navigation and path planning

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply various Robot Operating System (ROS) tools to model, simulate and visualize the development of various robotics systems.
- Evaluate ROS-based integration of robotic control and perception systems using sensors and cameras.
- Demonstrate the usage of basic robot navigation and path planning algorithms in mobile robots.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Prerequisite overridden by programme structure.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARL6143 Linear Systems & Signals.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARL6143

Title: Linear Systems & Signals

Aliases: Linear Systems and Signals

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: ARL6113 Electrical Circuits

Objective:
To develop skills for circuit and signal analysis as well as filter design.

Assessment:
- Test: 15%
- Assignment: 15%
- Lab Assessment: 10%
- Final exam: 60%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to signals
- Network topology
- Circuit analysis using Laplace transform
- State variable analysis of RLC networks
- Two-port networks
- Network synthesis of passive elements
- Passive filters

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Perform signal classification, signal modelling and convolution.
- Analyse electric circuits using network graphs.
- Compute network parameters for two-port networks.
- Solve for the time and frequency behaviour of electric circuits using Laplace Transform and state variable analysis.
- Construct one-port networks and filters.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Prerequisite overridden by programme structure.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARC6133 Advanced Programming.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARC6133

Title: Advanced Programming

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide undergraduates with a basic comprehension of the C++ programming language and the concepts of Object Oriented Programming (OOP).

Assessment:
- Test 1: 20%
- Test 2: 20%
- Assignment: 10%
- Final exam: 50%

Course Contents:
- Object oriented and C++ fundamentals
- Object classes
- Dynamic memory allocation
- Inheritance and polymorphism
- Basic OO design using UML
- Advanced C++ concepts and applications

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Create objects and classes with input/output using C++ code and UML class diagrams.
- Construct relationship between classes and objects using C++ code and UML class diagram.
- Evaluate the usage of function templates and class templates in a program.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.summary:
Subject Code: ARM6133

Title: Linear Algebra and Numerical Methods

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Contact Hours: 48 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Objective:
This course introduces students to the mathematical concepts and analysis methods of linear algebra, numerical methods and optimization for solving practical problems in science and engineering.

Assessment:
- Test 1: 15%
- Test 2: 10%
- Quiz: 5%
- Assignment: 10%
- Final exam: 60%

Course Contents:
- Linear algebra
- Numerical methods
- Optimization

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Use various methods in linear algebra to solve systems of algebraic equations.
- Apply numerical methods to solve mathematical problems including interpolation, differentiation, integration, root finding, ordinary differential equations and systems of linear equations.
- Determine the optimal solutions to the optimization problems using appropriate methods.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARR6123 Robotics – Machine Design and Mechanisms.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARR6123

Title: Robotics – Machine Design and Mechanisms

Aliases: Robotics - Machine Design and Mechanisms

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To develop the skills for designing linkage mechanisms and mechanical systems that contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Test: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Kinematics fundamentals
- Linkage synthesis
- Engineering materials
- Screws, fasteners and nonpermanent joints
- Bearings, couplings and lubrication
- Flexible mechanical elements

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe the fundamental of linkage mechanisms.
- Choose suitable engineering materials based on criteria such as strength, flexibility, machinability, cost and weight.
- Evaluate suitable mechanical systems which contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.
- Build a mechanical system which contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARL6153 Feedback Control.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARL6153

Title: Feedback Control

Aliases: Feedback control

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 45 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To introduce time domain, root locus and frequency domain methods for analysis and design of feedback control systems.

Assessment:
- Test: 15%
- Assignment: 15%
- Lab: 10%
- Final exam: 60%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to control systems
- System modelling
- Time domain analysis of control systems
- Root locus method
- Frequency domain analysis
- Compensation techniques

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply differential equations and transfer functions to the modelling of physical systems.
- Analyse transient response, error and stability of control systems using time domain and root locus methods.
- Analyse properties of control systems using frequency domain methods.
- Design compensators for control systems using strategic thinking.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARL6123 Electrical Machines and Power Systems.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARL6123

Title: Electrical Machines and Power Systems

Aliases: Electrical Machines and Power System

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 47 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To introduce the basic concepts and working principles of electrical machines and transformers as well as electrical power systems and their protection methods.

Assessment:
- Test: 10%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Assessment: 20%
- Final exam: 50%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to machinery principles
- Three-phase circuits
- Transformers
- D.C. machines
- AC machines (Induction Motors & Synchronous Motors)

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply the machinery principles and balanced three-phase power system circuit analysis on electrical machines and transformers.
- Analyze the circuit characteristics and performance of transformers.
- Analyze the circuit characteristics and performance of DC and AC machines.
- Demonstrate the characteristics and performance of single-phase transformer and induction motor.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Normalized title to plural 'Systems'.

children.summary:
Subject Code: ARP6110-P1

Title: Project I

Aliases: ARP6110, Project

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2028

Credit Hours: 5

Contact Hours: 35 hours

Pre-Requisite: Completed 60 credit hours (excluding Art & Humanities subjects)

Objective:
To enable students to apply knowledge and skills gained from different courses through solving of real world problems; acquire technical skills through literature review, analysis, design, implementation, and testing/evaluation; and develop soft skills needed to carry out a real world project.

Assessment:
- Part 1 – General Effort, Project Design, Research Proposal: 25%
- Part 1 – Presentation: 10%

Course Contents:
- Project planning, analysis and design
- Research proposal
- Presentation

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Develop the strategies for background study, analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation based on real-world problem.
- Demonstrate communication skills in writing and presenting the project design and implementation.
- Employ contemporary digital and numeracy tools to critically analyze problems in artificial intelligence related fields.
- Demonstrate self-awareness in continuous lifelong-learning skills.
- Apply integrated problem solving to propose a research paper/proposal for commercialization of the product.

- Normalized from single ARP6110 code in structure.

children.summary:
Subject Code: ARP6110-P2

Title: Project II

Aliases: ARP6110, Project

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2028

Credit Hours: 5

Contact Hours: 35 hours

Pre-Requisite: Completed 60 credit hours (excluding Art & Humanities subjects); Part 2 follows Part 1

Objective:
Continuation of project work through implementation, testing/evaluation, final report, research paper/commercialisation proposal, and presentation.

Assessment:
- Part 2 – General Effort, Project Implementation, Research paper/Commercialisation proposal: 25%
- Part 2 – Presentation: 10%
- Final Report: 30%

Course Contents:
- Project implementation and testing/evaluation
- Final report
- Research paper/commercialisation proposal
- Presentation

- Develop the strategies for background study, analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation based on real-world problem.

- Employ contemporary digital and numeracy tools to critically analyze problems in artificial intelligence related fields.
- Demonstrate self-awareness in continuous lifelong-learning skills.
- Apply integrated problem solving to propose a research paper/proposal for commercialization of the product.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARR6143 Robotics – Modelling and Control.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARR6143

Title: Robotics – Modelling and Control

Aliases: Robotics - Modelling and Control

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provides students with rigorous mathematical skills on modelling and control of robotic arm and similar manipulator.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Test: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Laboratory:
Control and automation lab, Robotic arms.

Course Contents:
- Representing position and orientation
- Robot modeling
- Forward and inverse kinematics
- Trajectory generation
- Velocity relationships
- Dynamic and control

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Compute the pose of a robotic arm given the configuration, links and angle of each joint.
- Deduce the joint angle of each robotic arm link given the required pose via inverse kinematic.
- Generate the trajectory for the end-effector of a robotic arm via joint-space or Cartesian space schemes using anticipatory thinking.
- Construct linear trajectory-following control scheme for robotic arm.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARR6153 Mobile Robots and Drones.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARR6153

Title: Mobile Robots and Drones

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: ARC6123 Micro-controllers & Micro-processors; ARR6123 Robotics – Machine Design and Mechanisms

Objective:
To acquire knowledge to design, construct and program mobile robots and drones.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab/Demonstration: 20%
- Project: 40%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to mobile robots and drones
- Wheeled robots
- Balancing and legged robots
- Drones

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Discuss the essential components and building blocks of mobile robots and drones.
- Construct mobile robots or drones for solving a practical problem.
- Develop mobile robots or drones using anticipatory thinking to perform a specific task.
- Demonstrate entrepreneurial skills by writing a proposal for commercialization of the product/solution.
- Demonstrate leadership, autonomy and responsibility in solving a practical problem.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Prerequisite overridden by programme structure.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for ARC6184 Making Embedded Systems.
children.summary:
Subject Code: ARC6184

Title: Making Embedded Systems

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2028

Credit Hours: 4

Contact Hours: 60 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: ARC6123 Micro-controllers & Micro-processors

Objective:
To introduce the best practice and the art of putting together an embedded system, from selection of processors, basic hardware requirements, to software design.

Assessment:
- Quiz: 20%
- Test: 20%
- Lab Test: 30%
- Project: 30%

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Creating a system architecture
- Getting on the hardware
- Inputs, outputs and timers
- Managing the flow of activity
- Communicating with peripherals
- Doing more with less
- PCB design and layout
- PCB fabrication, soldering and reworking

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe the hardware and software requirements of a robust embedded systems from datasheet.
- Construct an embedded controller with support circuit from scratch.
- Develop a software framework using systematic thinking for the embedded controller to perform a useful task.
- Design PCB layout for an electronic prototype.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Prerequisite overridden by programme structure.

children.title: What is ART6116 Industrial Training about?
children.summary: ART6116 Industrial Training is a industrial_training subject in the technical group. children.summary:
Subject Code: ART6116

Title: Industrial Training

Category: industrial_training

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2028

Credit Hours: 6

Contact Hours: 90 hours

Pre-Requisite: Completed 60 credit hours

Objective:
To expose students to the real working environment and get acquainted with organization structure, business operations and administrative functions; to gain hands-on experience in related fields; and to foster collaboration between industry and university.

Assessment:
- Performance assessed by company supervisor: 20%
- Logbook assessed by company supervisor: 20%
- Lecturer’s visit: 20%
- Oral Presentation: 20%
- Report: 20%

- Industrial training
- Passing all five assessment components separately to accumulate six credit hours

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Express technical experience and solutions in written or oral forms.
- Demonstrate the codes of ethics and the responsibilities of engineering practice.
- Use digital and numeracy skills or other relevant tools to solve problems.
- Demonstrate the industrial best practices and entrepreneur principles in a team.

Subject Code: LMPU3312

Group: university

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

- Leadership with professional integrity

children.title: Show the full subject outline for LMPU3192 Falsafah dan Isu Semasa.
children.summary:
Subject Code: LMPU3192

Title: Falsafah dan Isu Semasa

Aliases: Philosophy and Current Issues

Category: mpu

Group: university

Year: 1

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2026

Credit Hours: 2

Contact Hours: 26 hours

Pre-Requisite: Nil

Objective:
To equip students to think critically and logically, to debate analytically and methodically, and to respond more sensibly and judiciously to the different challenges of current issues and ideologies.

Assessment:
- Assignment 1: 30%
- Presentation: 20%
- Quiz: 20%
- Assignment 2: 30%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to philosophy
- Metaphysics
- Epistemology
- Ideology
- Philosophy in life
- Logic
- Psychology and sociology
- Decolonization
- Selected case study on philosophy and current issues

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain current issues based on philosophy, National Philosophy of Education and Rukun Negara.
- Explain current issues based on the main flow of thinking in various philosophies.
- Analyze current issues through a comparative perspective of philosophy as a basis for intercultural dialogue.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: LMPU3192 — Falsafah dan Isu Semasa.pdf

Subject Code: LMPU3142

Title: Bahasa Melayu Komunikasi 2

Category: mpu

Group: university

Year: 1

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2026

Credit Hours: 2

Contact Hours: 28 hours

Pre-Requisite: Tiada

Objective:
Untuk melatih pelajar antarabangsa berkomunikasi dalam bahasa Melayu asas yang meliputi situasi kehidupan harian.

Assessment:
- Kuiz: 30%
- Pembentangan: 40%
- Ujian: 30%

Course Contents:
- Bunyi dan sebutan
- Nombor
- Saya, kawan dan keluarga
- Persekitaran
- Situasi perbualan
- Percutian

- Menerangkan kandungan teks penuh yang menggunakan ayat mudah dan ayat berlapis.
- Bertutur dalam pelbagai situasi dengan menggunakan ayat mudah dan ayat berlapis.
- Menyusun idea secara kreatif dan sistematik dalam penulisan karangan pendek.

- International-student MPU-U1 option.

children.title: Show the full subject outline for LSSW6112 Sustainable Society.
children.summary:
Subject Code: LSSW6112

Title: Sustainable Society

Category: university_course

Group: university

Year: 3

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2028

Credit Hours: 2

Contact Hours: 28 hours

Pre-Requisite: None

Objective:
To equip students with a comprehensive understanding of Environmental, Social, and Corporate Governance (ESG) principles within the broader context.

Assessment:
- Quiz: 20%
- Project: 80%

Course Contents:
- Definition and significance of ESG
- Global ESG trends and standards
- Environmental aspects
- Climate change and business
- Sustainable resource management
- Social factors
- Corporate social responsibility
- Diversity and inclusion
- Corporate governance
- Ethical decision-making

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply the basic knowledge of ESG.
- Demonstrate caring, ethical, mindful, and sustainable attributes through active participation in the ESG project.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: LSSW6112 — Sustainable Society.pdf

children.title: Show the full subject outline for LDCW6123 Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.
children.summary:
Subject Code: LDCW6123

Title: Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers

Category: university_course

Group: university

Year: 3

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 41 hours

Pre-Requisite: None

Objective:
The course aims to introduce the knowledge and skills of the digital competence and its framework elements. It also exposes learners to the emergence of digital competence as a new approach and skills needed to manage daily life tasks and industry.

Assessment:
- Assignment 1: 30%
- Assignment 2: 30%
- Project: 40%

Course Contents:
- Digital competence and its framework
- Information and data literacy
- Communication and collaboration
- Digital content creation
- Safety in digital world
- Problem solving in digital world
- Distributed version control and Git workflows

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe measures for digital safety and solutions for common digital problems in a modern workplace environment.
- Demonstrate ability to work with digital information collaboratively through collaboration and communication tools.
- Build a program collaboratively with digital content.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf

children.title: What is this Intelligent Robotics course about overall?
children.summary: The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.
children.content:
Q: What is this Intelligent Robotics course about overall?
A: The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.
